title: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)
summary: CME00065 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic transcription of Eugen Kölbing's 1877 parallel edition of the Northern Homily Cycle Theophilus legend. Kölbing prints Harley MS 4196 (H) and the Vernon Manuscript (V) in full parallel streams and uses fire-damaged Cotton MS Tiberius E VII (T) selectively to correct and annotate H. A complete original scan of Englische Studien 1, a later photographic-reprint scan, the complete 710-canvas Vernon codex, and the complete 290-canvas Lambeth Palace Library MS 260 are cached. Exact work loci are mapped inside the complete manuscript bundles. Huntington HM 129 exposes only fourteen selected images with no overlap at the Theophilus locus, so that cache is explicitly partial and has no work-portion mapping. DIMEV's current fourteen-witness census is recorded separately from the three witnesses Kölbing actually used.
review_notes: The abstract medieval work, Kölbing's article, physical witnesses, complete-object facsimiles, later scholarly descriptions, the U-M encoding, and the checked-in XML artifact are distinct entities.
H and V are parallel editorial witness streams, not intrinsic chapters or versions that should be counted together as the work's length.
Caching a source for research does not grant permission to redistribute its images or publish them through print-on-demand services.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00065
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00065; the file is 77,813 bytes.
The XML has one DIV1, two sequential DIV2 witness streams, 1,524 L elements, 56 LG elements, 152 milestones, 99 notes, and 41 PB elements.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00065
entities.description: A manually keyed electronic text derived from Kölbing's printed H/V edition on pp. 38-57, not directly from manuscript images.
notes: The XML revision note dated 2016 says two prose fragments were removed from the beginning of the text.
A residual PB milestone for printed p. 16 precedes the encoded edition text beginning at p. 38.

entities.label: Eugen Kölbing, Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage (1877)
entities.description: The first publication of this Middle English Theophilus version. Its introduction occupies pp. 16-37 and its parallel H/V text pp. 38-57; a Nachtrag appears on p. 186 and corrections on p. 540.

bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing, 'Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage, mit einer Einleitung zum ersten Male herausgegeben,' Englische Studien 1 (1877): 16-57; Nachtrag 186; corrections 540.
bibliographic.title: Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage, mit einer Einleitung zum ersten Male herausgegeben

notes: Kölbing's closing dateline is Breslau, July 1876.
The Nachtrag acknowledges additional Northern Homily Cycle manuscripts and says the edition on pp. 38ff. is not yet a critical edition.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of the original Englische Studien 1 (1877)
entities.description: A complete 560-page scan of the original journal volume, including binding matter, Kölbing's full article, Nachtrag, and errata.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete scanned journal-volume PDF with metadata and scandata derivatives

bibliographic.citation: Englische Studien, vol. 1, first reprinting (New York and London: Johnson Reprint Corporation, 1967).

entities.description: A complete 554-page scan of the photographic reprint, with OCR, metadata, and scandata derivatives.

entities.label: London, British Library, Harley MS 4196 (H)
entities.description: Kölbing's complete H copy text. The Theophilus tale is embedded at the end of the homily for the thirteenth Sunday after Trinity.

date_statements.label: Manuscript date
date_statements.value: Fourth quarter of the fourteenth century to first quarter of the fifteenth century

physical_description.extent: 258 leaves plus flyleaves, pastedown, and stub; Northern Homily Cycle at ff. 1r-132r; Theophilus at ff. 113rb-117va

entities.label: London, British Library, Cotton MS Tiberius E VII, Part II (T)
entities.description: Kölbing's selectively collated T witness, closely related to H and damaged by the 1731 Cotton Library fire. The Theophilus text is imperfect because leaf edges and lines are lost.

holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Tiberius E VII, Part II

date_statements.label: Part II date
date_statements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

physical_description.extent: 281 folios in two volumes; Part II is ff. 127-281; Theophilus at ff. 221rb-225rb
notes: Damaged by fire in 1731.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript; V)
entities.description: Kölbing's complete V copy text in the monumental Vernon Manuscript. The work is headed 'Þe Story off Theofle' and occupies ff. 203vc-205rc.

physical_description.extent: Complete monumental codex; Theophilus at ff. 203vc-205rc

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of MS Eng. poet. a. 1
entities.description: A complete 710-canvas color digital object including covers, spine, flyleaves, blanks, all leaves, and adjacent works. No dedicated head, tail, or fore-edge views are supplied.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest and 710 JPEG images requested at width 1600 pixels

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Selden Supra 52
entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century paper manuscript containing the Theophilus legend at ff. 220-226.

notes: Contemporary English brown leather over boards.

entities.description: A mid-fourteenth-century parchment Northern Homily Cycle witness and the earliest surviving exact witness identified in the present census; Theophilus is at ff. 164v-174.

physical_description.extent: The whole manuscript contains the Northern Homily Cycle; Theophilus at ff. 164v-174

entities.label: Minneapolis, University of Minnesota, MS Z.822.N.81
entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century paper-and-vellum Northern Homily Cycle manuscript, tentatively localized just north of Hull. The work title appears on f. 127v and the text occupies ff. 128-137.

physical_description.support: Primarily paper with six vellum leaves
physical_description.extent: 216 folios; Northern Homily Cycle at ff. 13-145v and 149r-185v; Theophilus at ff. 128-137

notes: Written in Ireland.

entities.label: DIMEV record 10
entities.description: Current census record for the Northern Homily Cycle Theophilus legend, identifying fourteen witnesses, titles, loci, first line, verse form, subjects, and editions.

entities.label: Anne B. Thompson, The Northern Homily Cycle (2008)
entities.description: Modern specialist edition and introduction used for cycle-level identity, structure, authorship, dating, origin, and manuscript context. Its edited text does not include this omitted Theophilus item and it is not upstream of CME00065.

rights.component: Repository XML serialization and encoding contributions
rights.basis: No explicit license file was found in the pinned CME submodule; public access does not itself establish reuse terms for new encoding contributions.
The U-M collection's DPLA record applies the No Copyright - United States rights statement; local repository contributions remain a separate layer.
The article was first published in 1877; Internet Archive reports no visible copyright notice and NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the original-volume scan item.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and metadata
rights.basis: The underlying 1877 text is public domain in the United States, but the item does not state a distinct license for scan files.
notes: Local research caching is not itself permission to redistribute the scan or use it commercially.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and other applicable jurisdictions
rights.basis: The reprint carries a modern 1967 publication statement. No item-level reuse license was verified.
The IIIF manifest explicitly attributes the images under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International.
notes: The license is noncommercial; it does not authorize for-profit print-on-demand reuse. The medieval text itself is a separate public-domain layer.

rights.basis: The manifest metadata says only 'Rights: LPL' and provides no license URI or explicit redistribution permission.
notes: Do not redistribute or publish the cached images without Lambeth Palace Library's permission; underlying medieval text rights are separate.

rights.basis: No item-level image license was found. Huntington's general policy places copyright assessment and citation responsibility on the user and provides a route for notification or rights questions.
notes: Free download does not by itself establish unrestricted redistribution or commercial use.

rights.component: METS digital edition PDF and editorial matter
rights.basis: METS states that digital editions are for individual use and require permission for reprinting; a separate CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license applies to XML, not automatically to the PDF or manuscript images.

rights.jurisdiction: France, United States, and contractual use
rights.basis: The 1981 article remains modern scholarship; Persée terms limit document use to personal, private, scientific, or teaching contexts and exclude unlicensed commercial reuse.

rights.component: 1893 catalog volume and Internet Archive scan
rights.basis: The Internet Archive metadata explicitly applies the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark to this scan item.

rights.component: DIMEV database record, descriptions, and site HTML
rights.basis: Public access to the record supports citation and research use but no item-level license authorizing redistribution of the cached site HTML was verified.
notes: Facts about medieval witnesses are distinct from copying or republishing DIMEV's presentation and prose. Keep the local HTML cache private unless permission or a reuse license is confirmed.

rights.component: Images supplied through Cambridge University Library Imaging Services
rights.basis: CUL says supplied images are initially for noncommercial research or private study and that reproduction requires prior written permission.
notes: The same contractual caution applies to requested images of Dd.1.1 and Gg.5.31.

rights.component: Images supplied by University of Minnesota Archives and Special Collections
rights.basis: The library supplies copies for private study, scholarship, or research and treats permission to publish, contracts, and possible fees separately.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A Theophilus exemplum embedded in the Northern Homily Cycle and represented by divergent H and V manuscript classes
editorial_practices.representation: Complete H and V texts printed in parallel columns
editorial_practices.description: Kölbing extracts the exemplum from its homiletic codex contexts and prints both divergent texts completely because he concludes that reconstructing the archetype is not possible. The parallel layout is editorial representation, not an intrinsic two-part work structure.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Tiberius E VII text closely related to H but damaged by fire
editorial_practices.representation: Selected T readings in H's apparatus and occasional H correction
editorial_practices.description: Kölbing uses T only for obvious H errors and noteworthy variants rather than printing it as a third continuous text.
editorial_practices.scope: H stream and notes, printed pp. 38-57

editorial_practices.source_feature: Kölbing's German introduction, two parallel Middle English streams, notes, Nachtrag, and errata
editorial_practices.representation: The H and V streams and notes on pp. 38-57
editorial_practices.description: The electronic text omits the German prose introduction on pp. 16-37, Nachtrag, and volume errata as separate source sections; its 2016 revision note also says two prose fragments were removed from the beginning.

evidence.summary: States personal, private, scientific, and teaching use limits and restrictions on commercial or unlicensed site reproduction.

evidence.citation: H. L. D. Ward, assisted by J. A. Herbert, Catalogue of Romances in the Department of Manuscripts in the British Museum, vol. 2 (London, 1893), 739-740.
evidence.summary: Contemporary catalog descriptions of Harley 4196 and Cotton Tiberius E VII, including Theophilus loci, line counts, homily context, and Tiberius fire damage. The title page confirms volume 2 was published in 1893 despite generic Internet Archive date metadata.

open_questions.question: Can complete color physical-object facsimiles be obtained under usable terms for the eleven witnesses without verified complete public digital objects?
next_steps: Request complete physical objects rather than target folios alone, including covers, binding, spine, edges, flyleaves, blanks, and adjacent works, and identify the exact Theophilus locus separately.
Ask each repository for resolution, price, delivery format, publication, repository redistribution, and commercial print-on-demand terms before reuse.

open_questions.question: Exactly which manuscript features do Kölbing's italic letters and other typographic interventions represent in each H and V stream?
next_steps: Compare every italicized or supplied form in the original 1877 print against the mapped Vernon images and a requested complete Harley 4196 facsimile.
Keep any conclusions witness-specific and distinguish abbreviation expansion, supplied loss, normalization, and print emphasis.

open_questions.question: Can the Theophilus item's composition date and authorship be narrowed independently of disputed cycle-wide hypotheses?
next_steps: Review post-2008 Northern Homily Cycle scholarship while retaining the Austin-canon attribution and Carver's 1295-1306 date as hypotheses rather than facts.
